sMnocAYAAAAJ.keywords: Software Systems, IoT Security, Botnets, DDoS Mitigation, Internet-wide Scanning, Malware Analysis, TLS/HTTPS and PKI, Cloud and Virtualization Security, Certificate Misissuance, Industrial Control Systems Security
sMnocAYAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Michael Donald Bailey is a faculty researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on the intersection of **software systems** and **Internet-scale security and measurement**. His lab combines *large-scale measurement*, *systems design*, and *empirical security analysis* to study real-world threats and defenses. Central to his work are investigations of <u>botnets and distributed denial-of-service (DDoS)</u>, <u>Internet-wide scanning and measurement</u>, and the <u>ecosystems that underpin secure communications (e.g., TLS/PKI)</u>. Bailey’s publications emphasize reproducible, measurement-driven insights that inform both academic understanding and operational practice.

---

## Research Areas
Bailey’s research spans multiple, tightly related areas in software systems and network security. He is best known for empirical and measurement-driven studies of large-scale threats—most prominently work on the Mirai botnet and long-running analyses of DDoS amplification vectors—which combine network telemetry with protocol and system analysis. He has applied Internet-wide scanning to reveal systemic vulnerabilities (e.g., studies underpinning the analysis of Heartbleed and the development of search tools for scan-backed discovery), and has produced influential measurements of the HTTPS certificate ecosystem and certificate misissuance. Other topics in his corpus include automated malware classification and anti-analysis techniques, covert channels in virtualized/cloud environments, IPv6 deployment and security policy, and security failures in modern web and IoT ecosystems (voice assistants, in-browser cryptojacking, smart contracts). Across these areas he frequently pairs novel measurement methodologies (darknet monitoring, wide-scale scans, ground-truth datasets) with systems-building (detectors, linters, and defensive architectures) to both characterize problems and propose practical mitigations.

## Notable Works
- "Understanding the Mirai Botnet" (2017) — A high-impact retrospective analysis that reconstructed Mirai’s propagation and operational characteristics, providing foundational insights into how IoT devices powered massive DDoS attacks and informing subsequent defensive strategies.  
- "The matter of heartbleed" (2014) — An Internet-scale investigation of the Heartbleed vulnerability that quantified exposure, measured remediation behavior, and illustrated how rapid, empirical scanning can shape understanding of protocol-level failures.  
- "Automated classification and analysis of internet malware" (2007) — Early, influential work on automated analysis pipelines for malware that advanced scalable approaches to detection, labeling, and characterization of large malware datasets.

## Academic Background
Bailey is a seasoned researcher in computer science and software systems affiliated with Georgia Tech’s College of Computing. Over his career he has produced a body of highly cited, widely used measurement studies and systems papers and has contributed to community-shaping efforts such as the Menlo Report on ethics for ICT research. His work has appeared in top systems and security venues and has been repeatedly adopted by practitioners and researchers alike (evidenced by substantial citation impact and broad topical influence). He maintains an active research webpage and group at Georgia Tech (http://mbailey.cc.gatech.edu/), collaborates across academia, industry, and operational communities, and regularly participates in program committees, workshops, and community standards and best-practice discussions that bridge measurement, systems, and security.

sfuwsSwAAAAJ.keywords: Neuro-inspired Machine Learning, Network Science, Computational Science, Available Bandwidth Estimation, Active Network Measurements, HTTP Adaptive Streaming, Differentiated Services and QoS, Internet Topology and AS Ecosystem, BGP Routing Security and Scalability, TCP Throughput Prediction
sfuwsSwAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Constantine Dovrolis is a **Professor of Computer Science** at **Georgia Tech** (College of Computing), where he leads a research group that combines systems experimentation, measurement, and modeling. His work spans large-scale **network measurement** and **network science**, with a sustained emphasis on practical tools and methodologies for understanding Internet performance. More recently he has extended his interests into *neuro-inspired machine learning* and *continual learning*, bridging networking and learning systems. His signature contributions focus on <u>available bandwidth estimation</u>, <u>packet-dispersion and capacity measurement</u>, and <u>adaptive HTTP video streaming behavior</u> — research that produced widely used tools (e.g., **Pathload**) and influential measurement methodologies (e.g., **SLoPS**).

---

## Research Areas
Dovrolis’s research portfolio centers on empirical and analytic studies of network behavior, measurement methodology, and protocol/system design. He has developed and evaluated active measurement techniques for **available bandwidth** (see "Bandwidth estimation: metrics, measurement techniques, and tools" (2003) and the SLoPS methodology in "End-to-end available bandwidth: Measurement methodology, dynamics, and relation with TCP throughput" (2002)), and he has systematically characterized the limits and pitfalls of packet-dispersion methods ("What do packet dispersion techniques measure?" (2001); "Packet-dispersion techniques and a capacity-estimation methodology" (2004)). Another major thread is video delivery and adaptive streaming: his experimental evaluations of rate-adaptation algorithms ("An experimental evaluation of rate-adaptation algorithms in adaptive streaming over HTTP" (2011)) and investigations into player interactions and stabilization mechanisms ("What happens when HTTP adaptive streaming players compete for bandwidth?" (2012); "Server-based traffic shaping for stabilizing oscillating adaptive streaming players" (2013)) have shaped thinking about both client algorithms and server/CDN strategies. Complementing measurement work, he has studied Internet structure and economics (e.g., the evolution of the AS ecosystem in "Ten years..." (2008), "Twelve years..." (2011), and the shift toward a peering mesh in "The internet is flat" (2010)), routing and BGP robustness ("Beware of BGP attacks" (2004)), and infrastructure issues such as router buffer sizing and its performance trade-offs. More recently, his interests include **neuro-inspired continual learning** (e.g., "Nispa: Neuro-inspired stability-plasticity adaptation for continual learning in sparse networks" (2022)), demonstrating a willingness to apply network-science thinking and principled measurement to problems in machine learning and computational science.

## Notable Works
- "Bandwidth estimation: metrics, measurement techniques, and tools" (2003) — A comprehensive synthesis that framed the available-bandwidth problem for the research community, evaluated competing metrics and techniques, and motivated practical measurement tools; this work has been foundational for subsequent active measurement tools and studies (widely cited and influential).
- "End-to-end available bandwidth: Measurement methodology, dynamics, and relation with TCP throughput" (2002) — Introduced the **SLoPS** (Self-Loading Periodic Streams) methodology for end-to-end available bandwidth measurement and analyzed dynamics and relation to TCP performance; helped establish rigorous methodology for active probing.
- "An experimental evaluation of rate-adaptation algorithms in adaptive streaming over HTTP" (2011) — A systematic experimental study of adaptive video players that exposed key performance behaviors and trade-offs, informing both client adaptation design and CDN/server-side strategies for video delivery.

## Academic Background
Professor Dovrolis is a long-standing faculty member in the Georgia Tech College of Computing, with a prolific record of experimental, measurement-driven research and community-facing tools. His work has accumulated over sixteen thousand citations across topics in networking, systems, and, more recently, machine learning, reflecting broad impact across measurement methodology, Internet architecture, and multimedia delivery. He has contributed to community resources and measurement platforms (for example, involvement with Measurement Lab and multiple widely used measurement tools), mentored numerous graduate students and collaborators, and regularly publishes in top networking and systems venues. His interdisciplinary keywords — **Neuro-inspired Machine Learning**, **Network Science**, and **Computational Science** — capture both his traditional networking focus and his more recent engagements at the intersection of learning and complex systems.
